text: Patients with diabetes and CKD are at high risk of CKD progression and cardiovascular disease that requires a comprehensive strategy from a multidisciplinary team of health care professionals.

This approach should include a foundation of lifestyle modification and self-management (maintenance of a healthy plant-based diet, proper protein and sodium intake, optimal weight and exercise; absence of tobacco use), upon which are layered first-line drug therapies

HbA1c, glycated hemoglobin, is recommended to monitor glycemic control in patients with diabetes and CKD.
disagreement_detail: C:'hemoglobin' vs F:'HbA1c, glycated hemoglobin, is recommended to monitor glycemic control in patients with diabetes and CKD.'

text: Monitoring twice per year is reasonable and can be as often as 4 times per year if glycemic target is not met or there is a change in glucose-lowering therapy.

Precision and accuracy of HbA1c measurement declines with advanced CKD (G4-G5) since inflammatory states and metabolic acidosis can bias towards higher HbA1c while conditions such as anemia and transfusions that affect red blood cell survival or turnover can bias toward lower HbA1c.
disagreement_detail: C:'HbA1c' vs F:'Precision and accuracy of HbA1c measurement declines with advanced CKD (G4-G5) since inflammatory states and metabolic acidosis can bias towards higher HbA1c while conditions such as anemia and transfusions that affect red blood cell survival or turnover can bias toward lower HbA1c.'

text: For individuals in whom HbA1c measurements are not concordant with directly measured blood glucose or clinical symptoms, continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) can be used to index glycemia.
disagreement_detail: C:'HbA1c' vs F:'For individuals in whom HbA1c measurements are not concordant with directly measured blood glucose or clinical symptoms, continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) can be used to index glycemia.'

text: Self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) or the use of CGM can help monitor for episodes of hypoglycemia.

HbA1c target ranging from <6.5% to <8.0% is appropriate for patients with diabetes and CKD (not on dialysis) and should be individualized based on patient preferences, severity of CKD, presence of macrovascular complications or comorbidities, life expectancy, hypoglycemia burden, choice of glucose-lowering agents, and availability of resources or support system.
disagreement_detail: C:'HbA1c' vs F:'HbA1c target ranging from <6.5% to <8.0% is appropriate for patients with diabetes and CKD (not on dialysis) and should be individualized based on patient preferences, severity of CKD, presence of macrovascular complications or comorbidities, life expectancy, hypoglycemia burden, choice of glucose-lowering agents, and availability of resources or support system.'

A structured self-management education program is recommended for the care of people with diabetes and CKD, with considerations paid to local context, cultures, and availability of resources.

Self-management education programs should be operated within a framework of team-based integrated care promoted by policy-makers and institutional decision-makers that focuses on risk evaluation and patient empowerment as part of patients' comprehensive care.

Effective team-based integrated care should be supported and delivered by physicians and other allied health professionals such as trained nurses, dieticians, pharmacists, healthcare assistants, community workers and peer supporters.

text: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guidelines are based upon the best information available at the time of publication.
